summary: Spolsky endorses Dave Winer's philosophy that blog comments are counterproductive to free expression rather than enabling it. He argues that allowing comments beneath posts creates a torrent of anonymous noise, hate speech, and off-topic arguments that degrades the quality of discourse. Using vivid examples from a real estate blog where comments devolved into death threats and geopolitical flame wars, he makes the case that anonymity removes accountability and incentivizes the worst behavior. His proposed solution is that people should express disagreement on their own blogs, where they must take ownership of their words and earn attention through the quality of their ideas.
key_points: Blog comments infringe on the writer's freedom of expression rather than enabling free discourse — readers should get their own platform instead
Anonymity is the root cause of toxic comments, as people say things they would never say if they had to take ownership of their words
The quality of online discussion would dramatically improve if anonymous posting were eliminated entirely
The marketplace of ideas works better when people must earn attention through compelling writing and PageRank rather than piggy-backing on someone else's audience
Even innocuous topics like real estate devolve into hate speech and flame wars when anonymous commenting is allowed
money_quotes: You don't have a right to post your thoughts at the bottom of someone else's thoughts. That's not freedom of expression, that's an infringement on their freedom of expression.

Get your own space, write compelling things, and if your ideas are smart, they'll be linked to, and Google will notice, and you'll move up in PageRank, and you'll have influence and your ideas will have power.

What you get is a bunch of interesting ideas, carefully constructed, followed by a long spew of noise, filth, and anonymous rubbish that nobody … nobody … would say out loud if they had to take ownership of their words.
It's not fun, freewheeling freedom of expression, yay first amendment! It's mostly anonymous hate speech.
He's not even human to me, he's anonymous. What web browser he uses doesn't amount to a hill of beans.
The way to give people freedom of expression is to give them a quiet place to post their ideas.

Thoughtless drivel written by some anonymous non-entity who really didn't read the article very carefully and didn't come close to understanding it and who has no ability whatsoever to control his typing diarrhea if the site's software doesn't physically prevent him from posting.
key_insight: Blog comments don't enable free expression — they undermine it by letting anonymous voices drown out the carefully constructed ideas of the author, and true freedom of expression means giving people their own space to write, not a comment box on someone else's work.
